New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation Haleh Nourizadeh Kazerouni *

, Gabriel Toshiaki Tayama , Julie Fr´echette , Youn`es Messaddeq

Centre d’Optique, Photonique et Lasers (COPL), Universit´e Laval, Qu´ebec, Canada

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Membrane technologyAdditive manufacturing3D printingWater treatment

citation.title: New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation

content: A B S T R A C T We present a novel freeform fabrication strategy for polymer membranes via 3D printing, in which porosity is generated through a polymerization-induced phase separation mechanism. The approach employs a vatphotopolymerization compatible formulation to produce a macroporous network with coexisting meso­porosity. Hydrophilic flat-sheet membranes (400 μm thick) were fabricated and subjected to post-treatments involving CO2 supercritical drying and solvent exchange. The resulting membranes exhibited permeance of up to 36 and 29 LMH.bar-1, respectively, under a transmembrane pressure of 2 bar. An in-depth evaluation was conducted on membranes produced from photocurable formulations containing methacrylic acid (MA, 30 vol%) and polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA, 20 vol%), with varying ratios of 1-butanol and 2-phenoxyethanol as porogens. SEM analysis revealed a porous morphology throughout the membrane cross-section. The topperforming sample exhibited the highest specific surface area of 12.8 m2/g and the smallest mean pore di­ameters of 45 nm and 42 nm, as determined by adsorption branches of Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analyses, respectively. Pore size distribution was compared with liquid-liquid displacement method, which revealed mean pore size of 8.8 nm, with a small quantity of pores of approxi­mately 121 nm, which were in better agreement with pure water flux values.

citation.title: New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation

content: evealed mean pore size of 8.8 nm, with a small quantity of pores of approxi­mately 121 nm, which were in better agreement with pure water flux values. The influence of postpolymerization porogen removal methods—solvent exchange and supercritical drying—was also assessed. Post-treated membranes demonstrated lower compaction factors and more stable flux over time and under increasing pressure. This technique offers a promising route for the customized design of membrane geometries and architectures tailored to specific separation challenges. We further demonstrate such concept by printing textured membranes with wavy and rugged surface profiles.

citation.title: New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation

content: 1. Introduction The most common methods for preparing polymeric membranes are based on the phase inversion phenomenon, in which a polymeric solu­tion undergoes phase separation triggered by a specific mechanism, depending on the process. This trigger may involve a change in tem­perature (for polypropylene (PP) [1], polyethylene (PE) [2]), the introduction of a non-solvent (for polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) [3], polyvinyl chloride (PVC) [4], cellulose acetate (CA) [5]), or solvent evaporation (for polystyrene (PS) [6]). Polymeric membranes can exhibit different morphologies depending on the preparation approach [7]. In these methods, the polymer must be dissolved in large volumes of solvents, which are often highly toxic to both humans and the environment [8]. In water treatment applications in particular, persis­tent challenges such as membrane fouling and balancing membrane flux and rejection remains an issue, leading to increased operational costs and reduced efficiency. To address these challenges, current research is focusing on fabrication strategies that minimize solvent consumption [9], enabling more sustainable and environmentally friendly processes [10] while improving membrane performance and cost-effectiveness [11].

This technology enables innovative designs for membrane modules, oil/water separation

In particular, 3D printing shows great potential for the customized fabri­cation of membranes with complex geometries and integrated module configurations—capabilities unattainable with conventional methods such as phase inversion [13,14]. This exceptional design flexibility creates new possibilities to enhance mass transport, selectivity, and antifouling properties through precise hierarchical structural control. Researchers have successfully used 3D printing to fabricate wavy-supported composite membranes, demonstrating their superior performance over flat designs. In both ultrafiltration and nanofiltration applications, the wavy architecture proved highly effective at mitigating fouling and concentration polarization [15,16]. Other studies have further established that additive manufacturing (AM) enables the fabrication of ceramic membranes with architecturally complex and patterned surfaces, a feature that directly enhances filtration perfor­mance by improving hydrodynamics and antifouling properties [17,18].

citation.title: New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation

content: omplex and patterned surfaces, a feature that directly enhances filtration perfor­mance by improving hydrodynamics and antifouling properties [17,18]. However, the practical application of AM in membrane fabrication is still constrained by several technical limitations, such as inadequate nanoscale resolution, limited printable materials, slow printing rates, and high production costs. Ongoing advancements in photopolymerization-based technologies, coupled with the develop­ment of innovative photo-curable and composite materials, are expected to overcome these challenges. As a result, 3D printing is anticipated to play an increasingly important role in producing customized, high-performance membranes and may ultimately revolutionize mem­brane fabrication by integrating material design, structural engineering, and module manufacturing into a single, unified process [14,19]. While directly manufacturing porosity of the materials via AM is not feasible with current technologies, alternative strategies can be employed. One such approach is polymerization-induced phase sepa­ration (PIPS), a phase inversion process in which an initial monomersolvent solution of monomers and solvents undergoes phase separa­tion during polymerization. This phenomenon arises from the thermo­dynamic incompatibility between the growing polymer chains and the solvent, leading to the formation of polymer-rich and solvent-rich do­mains.

citation.title: New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation

content: thermo­dynamic incompatibility between the growing polymer chains and the solvent, leading to the formation of polymer-rich and solvent-rich do­mains. Ultimately, the solvent-rich regions are removed (e.g., via evaporation or solvent extraction), leaving behind a porous structure [20–22]. This approach offers the possibility to directly 3D print mem­branes for water filtration using polymerization-based AM techniques such as vat-photopolymerization (VPP). This 3D-printing method fab­ricates objects in a layer-by-layer fashion by selectively exposing discrete layers of a photosensitive resin to UV/blue light. This process triggers the localized photopolymerization of the resin, forming a solid material. Therefore, freeform porous structures and membranes are possible to manufacture using resins that can undergo PIPS. While this approach has been demonstrated to prepare sorbents, there is no demonstration of its use in directly manufacturing membranes for water filtration. For instance, Dong et al. 3D-printed monolithic adsorbents using dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMAEA) as a monofunctional monomer and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDMA) as a bifunctional monomer, to adsorb methyl orange from aqueous solutions [22]. Mason et al. developed a 3D-printed hierarchical nanoporous sorbent platform for cobalt recycling.

citation.title: New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation

content: o adsorb methyl orange from aqueous solutions [22]. Mason et al. developed a 3D-printed hierarchical nanoporous sorbent platform for cobalt recycling. They synthesized a novel methacrylate-functionalized bisdicyclohexyl acetamide receptor and copolymerized it with a commercial diacrylate and porogens, forming nanoporous materials through photoinitiated polymerization-induced phase separation. The resulting sorbent demonstrated selective bind­ing of CoCl2 over LiCl in simulated ethanolic battery leachates [23]. Resins for VPP process are composed of multifunctional monomers, photoinitiator, and additives, which can be fillers, solvents, and dyes (in some cases). The development of new compositions should achieve the required structural and functional properties of the desired parts [24], including considerations related to its 3D printability, such as surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and anisotropic behavior [25]. Particu­larly, in the case of resins for VPP that should undergo PIPS, the choice of solvent and monomer system is a primary concern, as the latter should be totally soluble in the former, but should present limited solubility once polymerized. Furthermore, the hydrophilicity of the materials is an important criterion for water filtration applications, as it promotes higher water permeation. Therefore, monomer candidates for VPP resins capable of undergo­ing PIPS for membrane fabrication should be highly hydrophilic.

citation.title: New approach to 3D-print methacrylic acid/polyethylene glycol diacrylate-based membranes based on polymerization induced phase separation

content: gher water permeation. Therefore, monomer candidates for VPP resins capable of undergo­ing PIPS for membrane fabrication should be highly hydrophilic. In this case, (meth)acrylic acid, and other monomers with a (meth)acrylate group attached to polyethylene glycol (PEG) fragment are excellent candidates [26], given their hydrophilic and biocompatible polymerized forms with proven safety profiles [27]. These monomers undergo polymerization via the well-known free radical photopolymerization mechanism and are well-suited for VPP processes. Particularly, the use of hybrid systems combining acrylate and methacrylate is more effective as the polymerization kinetics of methacrylate is considerably slower than acrylates, while a pure acrylate system can cause distortions in the printed object [28] due to high shrinkage and sensitivity to oxygen [29]. In this study, we introduce an innovative approach for manufacturing membranes with a controlled distribution of mesopores and macropores using 3D printing. We developed photosensitive resin formulations that not only ensure compatibility with the printing pro­cess but also enable the formation of porous architectures through the polymerization-induced phase separation (PIPS) mechanism. This technological synergy allows to overcome current resolution constraints and lays the foundation for a new generation of membranes with cus­tomizable porosity and geometries.

To validate the potential of this approach, we produced porous, hydrophilic flat-sheet membranes tailored for water treatment applications. A bespoke resin formulation was designed, incorporating methacrylate (MA), polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA), 1-butanol, and 2-phenoxyethanol—a biocompat­ible antimicrobial preservative widely used in cosmetic and biomedical applications [29]. We conducted a comprehensive investigation into the effects of resin composition, processing parameters—including post-treatment methods such as supercritical CO2 drying (SCD) and solvent exchange—and membrane compaction, in order to assess their influence on the structural characteristics and performance of the resulting membranes. 2. Flat-sheet membranes, 400 μm-thick, were fabricated via 3D-printed on a Prusa SL1 printer with a layer height of 50 μm. This layer thickness was selected as it provides an optimal balance between print resolution and fabrication speed, ensuring high-quality output while minimizing printing time. To remove unpolymerized monomers, the samples were immersed in IPA for 24 h prior to post-treatment. The printing process generated no material waste, as the unused resin remaining in the vat was collected and stored in a light-blocking container for future use.

content: 2.3. Post-treatment of membranes The printed membranes were stored in IPA to extract residual solvent and unpolymerized reactants. Initial testing was performed without solvent exchange (direct IPA-to-water filtration), followed by a gradual IPA-to-water replacement via 1-h immersion. However, this approach resulted in no measurable pure water flux (PWF), attributed to the membranes being soft and delicate, leading to structural pore collapse during the abrupt solvent exchange. To address this issue, two post-treatment strategies were implemented: Supercritical CO2 drying (SCD) – This method enabled complete solvent removal while minimizing capillary forces that damage pore structure Conventional solvent exchange – A more economical approach, used for comparative assessment of membrane performance. The impact of each treatment on membrane morphology and PWF was systematically evaluated. 2.3.1. Supercritical CO2 drying (SCD) A supercritical dryer (LADD CPD3, Ladd Research Industries, USA) was employed to prevent pore collapse in the 3D-printed membranes. Samples were kept in IPA while the dryer chamber temperature was lowered to 5 ◦C by quickly depressurizing CO2. When the chamber became cold enough, excess IPA was removed, and the samples were quickly placed in the chamber, which was then filled with CO2 to a pressure of at least 55.2 bar. A small outlet in the chamber allowed continuous CO2 flow for 45 min to purge any residual solvent.

The dried samples were transferred to a hermetic container and maintained at 40 ◦C to prevent moisture uptake until further analysis. 2.3.2. Solvent exchange In this study, a sequential solvent exchange protocol was developed to replace IPA in the membrane pores with deionized (DI) water prior to PWF testing. To minimize structural damage, alcohols of decreasing molecular size (IPA → ethanol → methanol) were used as intermediate solvents before the final water exchange. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the first step involved displacing IPA with ethanol. The subsequent replacements (ethanol → methanol and methanol → DI water) followed the same procedure, maintaining identical solvent ratios and step durations as in the initial IPA-to-ethanol exchange. 2.4. The analysis utilized an Isobutanol-Porefil liquid pair with an interfacial tension of 7.25 dyn/cm, a contact angle of 0◦, and a shape factor of 0.715. content: The contact angle of the membranes was measured using the captive bubble method, suitable for highly porous and hydrophilic materials. This technique ensures the solid sample reaches equilibrium with both the atmospheric interfacial tension and the liquid phase. It also mini­mizes contamination at the solid-vapor interface [30]. The samples were fully submerged and equilibrated with the aqueous phase before the test, preventing any liquid penetration during measurement and ensuring the measurement reflects the solid-water interface in its operational, hy­drated state. Then, they were mounted on a custom-made holder on a tilting stage, and an air bubble was injected beneath the membrane using a syringe pump (New Era Pump Systems Inc., USA) at a rate of 1.00 ml/min. Two measurement protocols were employed: a static captive bubble method and a dynamic method. In the dynamic protocol, the submerged sample holder was tilted, causing the air bubble to tra­verse the surface of the membrane. The angles at the bubble’s front (advancing, θa) and rear (receding, θr) were recorded to directly deter­mine contact angle hysteresis (CAH) as θr – θa. Images were collected using an industrial camera (DMK 33UX264, The Imaging Source, Ger­many) equipped with a macro lens (super multi coated macro takumar 1:4/50 mm, Asahi Opt. Co., Japan), and the contact angle between the air bubble and the membrane was measured using ImageJ.

PWF and compaction of the membranes Membrane filtration is a cost-effective water treatment technology, offering relatively low capital and operational expenses compared to other methods. Additionally, it is highly energy-efficient, which is crucial for large-scale operations. In this regard, a laboratory-scale crossflow filtration system was assembled using a commercial membrane holder (CF016A, Sterlitech, USA) equipped with a porous support plate to provide structural stability and ensure even pressure distribution during operation. The effective filtration area of the membranes used was 20.6 cm2, reported by the holder company. The system was con­nected to a vacuum pump (LIQUIPORT NF 1.300, Switzerland). PWF measurements were conducted at transmembrane pressures of 0.7, 1, and 2 bar without removing the sample between tests. All analyses were conducted at room temperature (25 ◦C). The PWF and permeance of each sample were measured twice and calculated using Equation (4)[32] and 5 [15], respectively: J = VA.t(4) Permeance =VA.t. Results and discussion 3.1. Physicochemical properties Raman scattering spectra of the prepared membranes and resins are presented in Fig. 2(a) and (b), respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 2(a), the observed vibrational bands are characteristic of acrylateand methacrylate-based polymers, with the distinguishable bands at 1726 cm-1, and 1697 cm-1 assigned to the symmetric stretching of the carbonyl group (C––O). The presence of the two carbonyl vibration bands is related to the formation of dimer species through hydrogen interactions. The vibrational bands centered at 1634 cm-1, and 1451 cm-1 are assigned to C––C stretching from unpolymerized acrylate/ methacrylate species, and asymmetric C–H bending in α-CH3 from methacrylate species, respectively. Skeletal C–C stretching is observed at 1123 cm-1 and 767 cm-1, while C–C skeletal bending is observed at 516 cm-1, and 597 cm-1. Bands centered at 1281 cm-1, 1243 cm-1, and 1200 cm-1 are assigned to C–O stretching modes and those at 968 cm-1, 930 cm-1, and 855 cm-1 correspond to rocking modes of the α-CH3 and CH2 [35] The Raman spectra of the resins, in Fig. 2(b), show additional fea­tures associated with the porogens 2-phenoxyethanol and 1-butanol. content: concentration of 1-butanol leads to higher resin viscosity, shorter UV exposure times for each layer, and a lower fraction of unpolymerized monomers. The higher viscosity hinders polymeriza­tion efficiency, allowing the gel point to be reached earlier in the pho­topolymerization process, causing the reaction to be diffusion-limited at earlier stages [38]. The increased viscosity also contributes to greater layer stability, helping the resin maintain its shape between exposures and enabling faster solidification with minimal deformation. Since 2-phenoxyethanol is significantly less volatile than 1-butanol, replacing the latter with the former reduces solvent evaporation during curing, resulting in a more stable resin system that requires shorter UV exposure for proper layer formation. SEM images of the flat-sheet membranes are presented in Fig. 3. The top row depicts surface morphologies, highlighting the influence of formulation on pore formation. It can be observed that increasing the concentration of 2-Phenoxyethanol while simultaneously decreasing the amount of 1-Butanol results in smaller surface pores with more homo­geneous distribution. This suggests that 2-Phenoxyethanol plays a role in stabilizing the polymerization process at the surface, producing a finer and more uniform morphology. These differences are primarily attributed to the distinct solvation effects of 2-Phenoxyethanol and 1Butanol.

Previous studies have demonstrated that the porogen compo­sition significantly influences the phase separation microstructure. Computational materials science facilitates the characterization of porescale microstructures via the integration of computer-aided techniques and digital reconstruction [39]. Using the mean-field Flory–Huggins theory [40], researchers computed the conversion phase diagram of the system and simulated the microstructure evolution during polymerization-induced phase separation through a Cahn–Hilliard-type phase-field model [22,39,41]. Cross-sectional SEM images of the membranes reveal a pronounced asymmetric structure from the bottom (first polymerized layer) to the top (last polymerized layer). The lower regions are denser and more compact, whereas the upper layers display a looser structure. This asymmetry likely arises from the sequential UV polymerization process, during which earlier layers continue to receive residual UV light expo­sure as subsequent layers are cured. Additional exposure drives further cross-linking in the initial layers, increasing their density and rigidity; however, this is counteracted at the surface by oxygen inhibition, a fundamental phenomenon in photopolymerization [42] that limits monomer conversion.

The resulting conversion gradient is evidenced by larger pores, thinner polymer structure, and smaller rods in the layers closer to the surface region [43,44]. Fig. 4 presents the surface morphology of the fabricated membranes, comparing 2D and 3D optical profilometry images with corresponding SEM micrographs. A clear morphological distinction is evident between the sample groups. The profilometry images for samples M1-M3 reveal a highly heterogeneous and textured topography, which is consistent with the SEM observations of a distinct thread-like structure, larger surface pores, and a non-uniform pore size distribution. Conversely, the images for samples M4-M6 indicate a significantly more uniform and smoother surface texture. These qualitative observations are quantitatively sub­stantiated by the surface roughness data presented in Table 3. Further­more, the significantly larger Sz (maximum height) values for M1-M3 confirm a greater peak-to-valley distance, indicating the pronounced topographic non-uniformity visible in the images. Table 3 presents the surface roughness characteristics of the fabri­cated membranes. The areal roughness parameters are defined as follows: Sa is the arithmetical mean height, representing the average roughness across the entire measured area. Sq is the root mean square height, a more statistically significant parameter sensitive to extreme peaks and valleys. Sz denotes the maximum height, defined as the sum of the height of the highest peak and the depth of the deepest valley within the assessment area. As the data indicate, the Sa values for all samples fall within a range of 1.5–3.8 μm. Samples M1-M3 exhibit the highest Sa and Sq values, signifying a greater overall surface roughness compared to samples M4-M6. This increased roughness is attributed to their fibrous and thread-like morphology, which, in conjunction with larger surface pore sizes as observed in SEM images, creates a more pro­nounced texture. Furthermore, the Sz results reveal a greater peak-tovalley distance for samples M1-M3. This suggests a more pronounced topographic non-uniformity, where the profilometer detects both higher protrusions and deeper pores compared to the topographically smoother surfaces of samples M4-M6. The BJH parameters include cumulative pore volume (VBJH) for pores between 1.7 and 300 nm, and average pore width (DBJH). These results are summarized in Table 3. Among all samples, M5 exhibited the highest SBET and the smallest pore diameter according to both BET and BJH analyses. Taken together, the isotherm characteristics – supported by the mean pore size values (Table 4), pore size distribution profiles (Fig. 6), and SEM observations – indicate that the membranes are predominantly, macroporous, with a secondary contribution from mesopores. The pore size distribution was further determined by Liquid-Liquid Displacement Porometry (LLDP), with the resulting flow-pressure curves presented in Fig. 7. The instrument’s automated analysis, which employs a fixed flow threshold of 3 μl/min to mitigate low-flow noise, reported a maximum pore diameter of 10.7 nm and a corre­sponding bubble point pressure of 19.3 bar. A detailed analysis of the wet curve, however, indicates that the initial permeation occurs at a significantly lower pressure of 1.7 bar.

Calculating the pore diameter from this pressure reveals the presence of a distinct population of larger pores, with a maximum diameter of approximately 121 nm. The mean flow pore diameter, defined by the intersection of the half-dry and wet curves, was 8.8 nm, while the smallest detectable pore diameter, iden­tified from the convergence of the wet and dry curves, was 7.8 nm. These results demonstrate a strongly asymmetric pore size distribution. Despite the existence of a small number of large defects (~121 nm), the membrane is predominantly characterized by a much smaller modal pore size, as evidenced by the mean pore diameter (8.8 nm) being much closer to the smallest detected pore (7.8 nm) than to the maximum. The difference between the mean pore sizes determined by LLDP (8.8 nm) and gas sorption methods (BET: 64 nm, BJH: 44 nm) arises from the fundamental differences in what each technique measures. LLDP characterizes the active, through-going pores in an intact mem­brane by detecting the pressure required to initiate fluid flow, thereby reporting the diameter of the narrowest constrictions, or throats, within the permeable pathways.

Consequently, gas sorption provides a volumetric distribution of the pore body sizes within the bulk material, which are inherently larger than the flow-limiting throats measured by LLDP. This confirms a membrane structure where larger internal voids are interconnected by significantly smaller pathways that control liquid movement. Post-treatment of membranes and PWF Fig. 8 shows the SEM images of the M4 sample before and after solvent exchange. The membrane before solvent exchange exhibits a more defined and interconnected network structure with visible fibrous or thread-like formations. As a result of its network arrangement, pores appear larger and elongated, suggesting a less dense structure. As can be seen from the image of the sample after solvent exchange, the membrane is denser and has a more granular texture. There are smaller, more irregular voids that look more compact, indicating a higher material density. As IPA (23 mN/m) has a lower surface tension than water (72 mN/m), capillary forces can act more strongly on the structure, which could collapse or densify the porous network during solvent exchange. Water could induce swelling or partial restructuring of the network. This might lead to a denser, more compact appearance.

content: The effects of solvent exchange and supercritical CO2 drying on membrane performance were investigated. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the membrane treated via solvent exchange exhibited a permeance of 58 LMH.bar-1 at 0.7 bar, which decreased with increasing pressure to finally reach 29 LMH.bar-1 at 2 bar. For the SCD-treated membrane, rewetted for 1 h before testing, the initial permeance was substantially higher at 108 LMH.bar-1 and decreasing to final point of 36 LMH.bar-1 as pressure rose. This behavior suggests that, at each pressure stage, the membrane experienced compaction, reducing permeance, until reach­ing close to the permeance of solvent-exchanged membrane. In contrast, the solvent-exchanged membrane exhibited lower compaction and less pronounced stretching, indicating greater structural stability during filtration. The reduced permeance of the 3D-printed membranes is primarily attributed to their significant thickness (400 μm), which introduces considerable hydraulic resistance. As observed from Fig. 9 (b), membranes without post-treatment exhibited higher permeance due to the presence of residual IPA within the pore structure but also underwent substantial compaction, with permeance reductions of 37 %, 22 %, and 44 % at 0.7, 1, and 2 bar, respectively. In contrast, solvent-exchanged membranes showed mark­edly lower permeance declines— 9 %, 7 %, and 9 %—under the same conditions, highlighting the effectiveness of solvent exchange in miti­gating compaction. This trend is reflected in the CF values, which ranged from 1.0 to 1.6, with untreated samples consistently exhibiting higher CFs. The SEM image of the post-treated sample by solvent exchange, corresponding to the data shown in Fig. 10 (a), is presented in Fig. 8. The surface fibers appear compacted under applied pressure, indicating a structural response to mechanical stress during filtration. Such compaction likely contributes to the observed reduction in water flux, as reduced pore interconnectivity and surface porosity can hinder water transport. The cross-sectional images further confirm this behavior, revealing densification across the membrane thickness, particularly near the top surface. This deformation is attributed to the membrane’s flex­ible polymer network, based on methacrylic acid and long-chain PEGDA (Mn 575).

Consequently, although solvent exchange improves stability, these results emphasize that further optimization of posttreatment is essential to mitigate compaction and enhance mechanical durability. 4. Limitation and future work This work establishes a platform with significant potential to advance membrane technology by fundamentally decoupling membrane design from traditional manufacturing constraints. As shown in Fig. 11, the demonstrated ability to fabricate membranes with customized, complex textures and architectures opens a new paradigm for creating application-specific membranes, where surface topography and pore geometry can be actively engineered to control fouling, flux, and selectivity. Conclusion Membranes were successfully fabricated via 3D printing using a resin formulation composed of methacrylic acid (MA) and polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA) as the monomer system, with 1-butanol and 2-phe­noxyethanol serving as porogens. SEM revealed an asymmetric crosssectional architecture and surface pores in the range of 50–100 nm, which was further corroborated by BET analysis. BET results confirmed that the membranes were predominantly macroporous, with a minor fraction of mesopores. Although the mean pore diameter for all samples was below 100 nm, pore size distribution analysis demonstrated that samples M4, M5, and M6 exhibited a distinct peak below 100 nm, sug­gesting a higher volume of smaller pores relative to other formulations. Captive bubble point contact angle measurements demonstrated high hydrophilicity for all samples, with values ranging from 27◦to 34◦.
